question:
10. Given the power-series
\[
a_{0}+a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+\cdots
\]
in which
\[
a_{n}=\left(n^{2}+1\right) 3^{n}
\]
show that there is a relation of the form
\[
a_{n}+p a_{n+1}+q a_{n+2}+r a_{n+3}=0
\]
in which \( p, q, r \) are constants independent of \( n \). Find these constants and the sum of the power-series.
solution:
First Solution. The desired relation is
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(n^{2}+1\right) 3^{n}+p\left((n+1)^{2}+1\right) 3^{n+1} \\
+q\left((n+2)^{2}+1\right) 3^{n+2}+r\left((n+3)^{2}+1\right) 3^{n+3}=0
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
n^{2}(1+3 p+9 q+27 r)+n(6 p+36 q+162 r) \\
\quad+(1+6 p+45 q+270 r)=0
\end{array}
\]

Equation (1) holds for all \( n \) if and only if
\[
\begin{array}{r}
1+3 p+9 q+27 r=0 \\
p+6 q+27 r=0 \\
1+6 p+45 q+270 r=0
\end{array}
\]

These linear equations have the solution \( p=-1, q=\frac{1}{3}, r=-\frac{1}{27} \), so
\[
a_{n}-a_{n+1}+\frac{1}{3} a_{n+2}-\frac{1}{27} a_{n+3}=0
\]

Let \( S(x)=a_{0}+a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+\cdots \). Proceeding formally, we have
\[
\begin{array}{rlrl}
x^{3} S(x) & = & a_{0} x^{3}+a_{1} x^{4}+\cdots+a_{n-3} x^{n}+\cdots \\
p x^{2} S(x) & = & p a_{0} x^{2}+p a_{1} x^{3}+p a_{2} x^{4}+\cdots+p a_{n-2} x^{n}+\cdots \\
q x S(x) & = & q a_{0} x+q a_{1} x^{2}+q a_{2} x^{3}+q a_{3} x^{4}+\cdots+q a_{n-1} x^{n}+\cdots \\
r S(x) & =r a_{0}+r a_{1} x+r a_{2} x^{2}+r a_{3} x^{3}+r a_{4} x^{4}+\cdots+r a_{n} x^{n}+\cdots
\end{array}
\]

When we sum these we get
\[
S(x)\left[x^{3}+p x^{2}+q x+r\right]=\left(p a_{0}+q a_{1}+r a_{2}\right) x^{2}+\left(q a_{0}+r a_{1}\right) x+r a_{0}
\]

Multiplying through by -27 , we obtain
\[
S(x)\left[1-9 x+27 x^{2}-27 x^{3}\right]=1-3 x+18 x^{2}
\]
and therefore
\[
S(x)=\frac{1-3 x+18 x^{2}}{(1-3 x)^{3}}
\]

Using the ratio test we conclude that the series converges for \( |x|<\frac{1}{3} \); hence the formal manipulations above are valid for these values of \( x \).

Second Solution. Let \( b_{n}=a_{n} / 3^{n}=n^{2}+1 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta b_{n} & =b_{n+1}-b_{n}=2 n+1, \quad \Delta^{2} b_{n}=b_{n+2}-2 b_{n+1}+b_{n}=2 \\
\Delta^{3} b_{n} & =b_{n+3}-3 b_{n+2}+3 b_{n+1}-b_{n}=0
\end{aligned}
\]

So
\[
\frac{a_{n+3}}{3^{n+3}}-\frac{3 a_{n+2}}{3^{n+2}}+\frac{3 a_{n+1}}{3^{n+1}}-\frac{a_{n}}{3^{n}}=0
\]
whence
\[
a_{n}-a_{n+1}+(1 / 3) a_{n+2}-(1 / 27) a_{n+3}=0
\]

Since
\[
n^{2}+1=(n+1)(n+2)-3(n+1)+2
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma b_{n} y^{n} & =\Sigma(n+1)(n+2) y^{n}-3 \Sigma(n+1) y^{n}+2 \Sigma y^{n} \\
& =\frac{2}{(1-y)^{3}}-\frac{3}{(1-y)^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-y} \\
& =\frac{1-y+2 y^{2}}{(1-y)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
\]
provided \( |y|<1 \). Replace \( y \) by \( 3 x \).
\[
\Sigma a_{n} x^{n}=\frac{1-3 x+18 x^{2}}{(1-3 x)^{3}}
\]
provided \( |x|<\frac{1}{3} \).
Remark. We could assume that the problem is concerned with the ring of formal power series. In that case, \( (1-3 x) \) has an inverse in the ring and our result is that
\[
\sum a_{n} x^{n}=\left(1-3 x+18 x^{2}\right)(1-3 x)^{-3}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
10. Given the power-series
\[
coefzero+coefone \, variablex+coeftwo \, variablex^{2}+\cdots
\]
in which
\[
coefgeneral=\left(indexvar^{2}+1\right) 3^{indexvar}
\]
show that there is a relation of the form
\[
coefgeneral+constpval \, coefgennext+constqval \, coefgenplus2+constrval \, coefgenplus3=0
\]
in which \( constpval, constqval, constrval \) are constants independent of \( indexvar \). Find these constants and the sum of the power-series.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. The desired relation is
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(indexvar^{2}+1\right) 3^{indexvar}+constpval\left((indexvar+1)^{2}+1\right) 3^{indexvar+1} \\
+constqval\left((indexvar+2)^{2}+1\right) 3^{indexvar+2}+constrval\left((indexvar+3)^{2}+1\right) 3^{indexvar+3}=0
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
indexvar^{2}(1+3 constpval+9 constqval+27 constrval)+indexvar(6 constpval+36 constqval+162 constrval) \\
\quad+(1+6 constpval+45 constqval+270 constrval)=0
\end{array}
\]

Equation (1) holds for all \( indexvar \) if and only if
\[
\begin{array}{r}
1+3 constpval+9 constqval+27 constrval=0 \\
constpval+6 constqval+27 constrval=0 \\
1+6 constpval+45 constqval+270 constrval=0
\end{array}
\]

These linear equations have the solution \( constpval=-1, constqval=\frac{1}{3}, constrval=-\frac{1}{27} \), so
\[
coefgeneral-coefgennext+\frac{1}{3} coefgenplus2-\frac{1}{27} coefgenplus3=0
\]

Let \( seriesfunc(variablex)=coefzero+coefone \, variablex+coeftwo \, variablex^{2}+\cdots \). Proceeding formally, we have
\[
\begin{array}{rlrl}
variablex^{3} seriesfunc(variablex) & = & coefzero \, variablex^{3}+coefone \, variablex^{4}+\cdots+coefgenminus3 \, variablex^{indexvar}+\cdots \\
constpval \, variablex^{2} seriesfunc(variablex) & = & constpval \, coefzero \, variablex^{2}+constpval \, coefone \, variablex^{3}+constpval \, coeftwo \, variablex^{4}+\cdots+constpval \, coefgenminus2 \, variablex^{indexvar}+\cdots \\
constqval \, variablex \, seriesfunc(variablex) & = & constqval \, coefzero \, variablex+constqval \, coefone \, variablex^{2}+constqval \, coeftwo \, variablex^{3}+constqval \, coefthree \, variablex^{4}+\cdots+constqval \, coefgenminus1 \, variablex^{indexvar}+\cdots \\
constrval \, seriesfunc(variablex) & = & constrval \, coefzero+constrval \, coefone \, variablex+constrval \, coeftwo \, variablex^{2}+constrval \, coefthree \, variablex^{3}+constrval \, coeffour \, variablex^{4}+\cdots+constrval \, coefgeneral \, variablex^{indexvar}+\cdots
\end{array}
\]

When we sum these we get
\[
seriesfunc(variablex)\left[variablex^{3}+constpval \, variablex^{2}+constqval \, variablex+constrval\right]=\left(constpval \, coefzero+constqval \, coefone+constrval \, coeftwo\right) variablex^{2}+\left(constqval \, coefzero+constrval \, coefone\right) variablex+constrval \, coefzero
\]

Multiplying through by -27 , we obtain
\[
seriesfunc(variablex)\left[1-9 variablex+27 variablex^{2}-27 variablex^{3}\right]=1-3 variablex+18 variablex^{2}
\]
and therefore
\[
seriesfunc(variablex)=\frac{1-3 variablex+18 variablex^{2}}{(1-3 variablex)^{3}}
\]

Using the ratio test we conclude that the series converges for \( |variablex|<\frac{1}{3} \); hence the formal manipulations above are valid for these values of \( variablex \).

Second Solution. Let \( altcoefgeneral=coefgeneral / 3^{indexvar}=indexvar^{2}+1 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta altcoefgeneral & =altcoefgennext-altcoefgeneral=2 indexvar+1, \quad \Delta^{2} altcoefgeneral=altcoefgenplus2-2 altcoefgennext+altcoefgeneral=2 \\
\Delta^{3} altcoefgeneral & =altcoefgenplus3-3 altcoefgenplus2+3 altcoefgennext-altcoefgeneral=0
\end{aligned}
\]

So
\[
\frac{coefgenplus3}{3^{indexvar+3}}-\frac{3 coefgenplus2}{3^{indexvar+2}}+\frac{3 coefgennext}{3^{indexvar+1}}-\frac{coefgeneral}{3^{indexvar}}=0
\]
whence
\[
coefgeneral-coefgennext+(1 / 3) coefgenplus2-(1 / 27) coefgenplus3=0
\]

Since
\[
indexvar^{2}+1=(indexvar+1)(indexvar+2)-3(indexvar+1)+2
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma altcoefgeneral \, variabley^{indexvar} & =\Sigma(indexvar+1)(indexvar+2) \, variabley^{indexvar}-3 \Sigma(indexvar+1) \, variabley^{indexvar}+2 \Sigma \, variabley^{indexvar} \\
& =\frac{2}{(1-variabley)^{3}}-\frac{3}{(1-variabley)^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-variabley} \\
& =\frac{1-variabley+2 variabley^{2}}{(1-variabley)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
\]
provided \( |variabley|<1 \). Replace \( variabley \) by \( 3 \, variablex \).
\[
\Sigma coefgeneral \, variablex^{indexvar}=\frac{1-3 variablex+18 variablex^{2}}{(1-3 variablex)^{3}}
\]
provided \( |variablex|<\frac{1}{3} \).
Remark. We could assume that the problem is concerned with the ring of formal power series. In that case, \( (1-3 variablex) \) has an inverse in the ring and our result is that
\[
\sum coefgeneral \, variablex^{indexvar}=\left(1-3 variablex+18 variablex^{2}\right)(1-3 variablex)^{-3}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
10. Given the power-series
\[
raincloud+snowflake sandstone+thunderstorm sandstone^{2}+\cdots
\]
in which
\[
treeladder=\left(fireplace^{2}+1\right) 3^{fireplace}
\]
show that there is a relation of the form
\[
treeladder+sunflower riverstone+moonstone canyonpass+oceanbreeze meadowlark=0
\]
in which \( sunflower, moonstone, oceanbreeze \) are constants independent of \( fireplace \). Find these constants and the sum of the power-series.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. The desired relation is
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(fireplace^{2}+1\right) 3^{fireplace}+sunflower\left((fireplace+1)^{2}+1\right) 3^{fireplace+1} \\
+moonstone\left((fireplace+2)^{2}+1\right) 3^{fireplace+2}+oceanbreeze\left((fireplace+3)^{2}+1\right) 3^{fireplace+3}=0
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
fireplace^{2}(1+3 sunflower+9 moonstone+27 oceanbreeze)+fireplace(6 sunflower+36 moonstone+162 oceanbreeze) \\
\quad+(1+6 sunflower+45 moonstone+270 oceanbreeze)=0
\end{array}
\]

Equation (1) holds for all \( fireplace \) if and only if
\[
\begin{array}{r}
1+3 sunflower+9 moonstone+27 oceanbreeze=0 \\
sunflower+6 moonstone+27 oceanbreeze=0 \\
1+6 sunflower+45 moonstone+270 oceanbreeze=0
\end{array}
\]

These linear equations have the solution \( sunflower=-1, moonstone=\frac{1}{3}, oceanbreeze=-\frac{1}{27} \), so
\[
treeladder-riverstone+\frac{1}{3} canyonpass-\frac{1}{27} meadowlark=0
\]

Let \( breadcrumb(sandstone)=raincloud+snowflake sandstone+thunderstorm sandstone^{2}+\cdots \). Proceeding formally, we have
\[
\begin{array}{rlrl}
sandstone^{3} breadcrumb(sandstone) & = & raincloud sandstone^{3}+snowflake sandstone^{4}+\cdots+fernshadow sandstone^{fireplace}+\cdots \\
sunflower sandstone^{2} breadcrumb(sandstone) & = & sunflower raincloud sandstone^{2}+sunflower snowflake sandstone^{3}+sunflower thunderstorm sandstone^{4}+\cdots+sunflower cliffhaven sandstone^{fireplace}+\cdots \\
moonstone sandstone breadcrumb(sandstone) & = & moonstone raincloud sandstone+moonstone snowflake sandstone^{2}+moonstone thunderstorm sandstone^{3}+moonstone hillcrest sandstone^{4}+\cdots+moonstone brookfield sandstone^{fireplace}+\cdots \\
oceanbreeze breadcrumb(sandstone) & =oceanbreeze raincloud+oceanbreeze snowflake sandstone+oceanbreeze thunderstorm sandstone^{2}+oceanbreeze hillcrest sandstone^{3}+oceanbreeze stargazer sandstone^{4}+\cdots+oceanbreeze treeladder sandstone^{fireplace}+\cdots
\end{array}
\]

When we sum these we get
\[
breadcrumb(sandstone)\left[sandstone^{3}+sunflower sandstone^{2}+moonstone sandstone+oceanbreeze\right]=\left(sunflower raincloud+moonstone snowflake+oceanbreeze thunderstorm\right) sandstone^{2}+\left(moonstone raincloud+oceanbreeze snowflake\right) sandstone+oceanbreeze raincloud
\]

Multiplying through by -27 , we obtain
\[
breadcrumb(sandstone)\left[1-9 sandstone+27 sandstone^{2}-27 sandstone^{3}\right]=1-3 sandstone+18 sandstone^{2}
\]
and therefore
\[
breadcrumb(sandstone)=\frac{1-3 sandstone+18 sandstone^{2}}{(1-3 sandstone)^{3}}
\]

Using the ratio test we conclude that the series converges for \( |sandstone|<\frac{1}{3} \); hence the formal manipulations above are valid for these values of \( sandstone \).

Second Solution. Let \( lanternfly=\frac{treeladder}{3^{fireplace}}=fireplace^{2}+1 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta lanternfly & =emberglow-lanternfly=2 fireplace+1, \quad \Delta^{2} lanternfly=nightshade-2 emberglow+lanternfly=2 \\
\Delta^{3} lanternfly & =silverpine-3 nightshade+3 emberglow-lanternfly=0
\end{aligned}
\]

So
\[
\frac{meadowlark}{3^{fireplace+3}}-\frac{3 canyonpass}{3^{fireplace+2}}+\frac{3 riverstone}{3^{fireplace+1}}-\frac{treeladder}{3^{fireplace}}=0
\]
whence
\[
treeladder-riverstone+(1 / 3) canyonpass-(1 / 27) meadowlark=0
\]

Since
\[
fireplace^{2}+1=(fireplace+1)(fireplace+2)-3(fireplace+1)+2
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma lanternfly driftwood^{fireplace} & =\Sigma(fireplace+1)(fireplace+2) driftwood^{fireplace}-3 \Sigma(fireplace+1) driftwood^{fireplace}+2 \Sigma driftwood^{fireplace} \\
& =\frac{2}{(1-driftwood)^{3}}-\frac{3}{(1-driftwood)^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-driftwood} \\
& =\frac{1-driftwood+2 driftwood^{2}}{(1-driftwood)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
\]
provided \( |driftwood|<1 \). Replace \( driftwood \) by \( 3 sandstone \).
\[
\Sigma treeladder sandstone^{fireplace}=\frac{1-3 sandstone+18 sandstone^{2}}{(1-3 sandstone)^{3}}
\]
provided \( |sandstone|<\frac{1}{3} \).
Remark. We could assume that the problem is concerned with the ring of formal power series. In that case, \( (1-3 sandstone) \) has an inverse in the ring and our result is that
\[
\sum treeladder sandstone^{fireplace}=\left(1-3 sandstone+18 sandstone^{2}\right)(1-3 sandstone)^{-3}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
10. Given the power-series
\[
uncertainzero+uncertainone constant+uncertaintwo constant^{2}+\cdots
\]
in which
\[
uncertainindex=\left(endpoint^{2}+1\right) 3^{endpoint}
\]
show that there is a relation of the form
\[
uncertainindex+staticcoef uncertainplusone+stillcoef uncertainplustwo+rigidcoef uncertainplusthree=0
\]
in which \( staticcoef, stillcoef, rigidcoef \) are constants independent of \( endpoint \). Find these constants and the sum of the power-series.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. The desired relation is
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(endpoint^{2}+1\right) 3^{endpoint}+staticcoef\left((endpoint+1)^{2}+1\right) 3^{endpoint+1} \\
+stillcoef\left((endpoint+2)^{2}+1\right) 3^{endpoint+2}+rigidcoef\left((endpoint+3)^{2}+1\right) 3^{endpoint+3}=0
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
endpoint^{2}(1+3 staticcoef+9 stillcoef+27 rigidcoef)+endpoint(6 staticcoef+36 stillcoef+162 rigidcoef) \\
\quad+(1+6 staticcoef+45 stillcoef+270 rigidcoef)=0
\end{array}
\]

Equation (1) holds for all \( endpoint \) if and only if
\[
\begin{array}{r}
1+3 staticcoef+9 stillcoef+27 rigidcoef=0 \\
staticcoef+6 stillcoef+27 rigidcoef=0 \\
1+6 staticcoef+45 stillcoef+270 rigidcoef=0
\end{array}
\]

These linear equations have the solution \( staticcoef=-1, stillcoef=\frac{1}{3}, rigidcoef=-\frac{1}{27} \), so
\[
uncertainindex-uncertainplusone+\frac{1}{3} uncertainplustwo-\frac{1}{27} uncertainplusthree=0
\]

Let \( difference(constant)=uncertainzero+uncertainone constant+uncertaintwo constant^{2}+\cdots \). Proceeding formally, we have
\[
\begin{array}{rlrl}
constant^{3} difference(constant) & = & uncertainzero constant^{3}+uncertainone constant^{4}+\cdots+uncertainminusthree constant^{endpoint}+\cdots \\
staticcoef constant^{2} difference(constant) & = & staticcoef uncertainzero constant^{2}+staticcoef uncertainone constant^{3}+staticcoef uncertaintwo constant^{4}+\cdots+staticcoef uncertainminustwo constant^{endpoint}+\cdots \\
stillcoef constant difference(constant) & = & stillcoef uncertainzero constant+stillcoef uncertainone constant^{2}+stillcoef uncertaintwo constant^{3}+stillcoef uncertainthree constant^{4}+\cdots+stillcoef uncertainminusone constant^{endpoint}+\cdots \\
rigidcoef difference(constant) & = & rigidcoef uncertainzero+rigidcoef uncertainone constant+rigidcoef uncertaintwo constant^{2}+rigidcoef uncertainthree constant^{3}+rigidcoef uncertainfour constant^{4}+\cdots+rigidcoef uncertainindex constant^{endpoint}+\cdots
\end{array}
\]

When we sum these we get
\[
difference(constant)\left[constant^{3}+staticcoef constant^{2}+stillcoef constant+rigidcoef\right]=\left(staticcoef uncertainzero+stillcoef uncertainone+rigidcoef uncertaintwo\right) constant^{2}+\left(stillcoef uncertainzero+rigidcoef uncertainone\right) constant+rigidcoef uncertainzero
\]

Multiplying through by -27 , we obtain
\[
difference(constant)\left[1-9 constant+27 constant^{2}-27 constant^{3}\right]=1-3 constant+18 constant^{2}
\]
and therefore
\[
difference(constant)=\frac{1-3 constant+18 constant^{2}}{(1-3 constant)^{3}}
\]

Using the ratio test we conclude that the series converges for \( |constant|<\frac{1}{3} \); hence the formal manipulations above are valid for these values of \( constant \).

Second Solution. Let \( volatileindex=uncertainindex / 3^{endpoint}=endpoint^{2}+1 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta volatileindex & =volatileplusone-volatileindex=2 endpoint+1, \quad \Delta^{2} volatileindex=volatileplustwo-2 volatileplusone+volatileindex=2 \\
\Delta^{3} volatileindex & =volatileplusthree-3 volatileplustwo+3 volatileplusone-volatileindex=0
\end{aligned}
\]

So
\[
\frac{uncertainplusthree}{3^{endpoint+3}}-\frac{3 uncertainplustwo}{3^{endpoint+2}}+\frac{3 uncertainplusone}{3^{endpoint+1}}-\frac{uncertainindex}{3^{endpoint}}=0
\]
whence
\[
uncertainindex-uncertainplusone+(1 / 3) uncertainplustwo-\left(1 / 27\right) uncertainplusthree=0
\]

Since
\[
endpoint^{2}+1=(endpoint+1)(endpoint+2)-3(endpoint+1)+2
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma volatileindex steadyval^{endpoint} & =\Sigma(endpoint+1)(endpoint+2) steadyval^{endpoint}-3 \Sigma(endpoint+1) steadyval^{endpoint}+2 \Sigma steadyval^{endpoint} \\
& =\frac{2}{(1-steadyval)^{3}}-\frac{3}{(1-steadyval)^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-steadyval} \\
& =\frac{1-steadyval+2 steadyval^{2}}{(1-steadyval)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
\]
provided \( |steadyval|<1 \). Replace \( steadyval \) by \( 3 constant \).
\[
\Sigma uncertainindex constant^{endpoint}=\frac{1-3 constant+18 constant^{2}}{(1-3 constant)^{3}}
\]
provided \( |constant|<\frac{1}{3} \).
Remark. We could assume that the problem is concerned with the ring of formal power series. In that case, \( (1-3 constant) \) has an inverse in the ring and our result is that
\[
\sum uncertainindex constant^{endpoint}=\left(1-3 constant+18 constant^{2}\right)(1-3 constant)^{-3}
\]

garbled_string.question:
10. Given the power-series
\[
lakdjfgh+qwerpoiu qzxwvtnp+zmxncvas qzxwvtnp^{2}+\cdots
\]
in which
\[
asdfghjk=\left(fpldsear^{2}+1\right) 3^{fpldsear}
\]
show that there is a relation of the form
\[
asdfghjk+rtyuioop ghjklasd+fghjklzx uioplkjh+vcxzlkjh xcvbnmas=0
\]
in which \( rtyuioop, fghjklzx, vcxzlkjh \) are constants independent of \( fpldsear \). Find these constants and the sum of the power-series.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. The desired relation is
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(fpldsear^{2}+1\right) 3^{fpldsear}+rtyuioop\left((fpldsear+1)^{2}+1\right) 3^{fpldsear+1} \\
+fghjklzx\left((fpldsear+2)^{2}+1\right) 3^{fpldsear+2}+vcxzlkjh\left((fpldsear+3)^{2}+1\right) 3^{fpldsear+3}=0
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
fpldsear^{2}(1+3 rtyuioop+9 fghjklzx+27 vcxzlkjh)+fpldsear(6 rtyuioop+36 fghjklzx+162 vcxzlkjh) \\
\quad+(1+6 rtyuioop+45 fghjklzx+270 vcxzlkjh)=0
\end{array}
\]

Equation (1) holds for all \( fpldsear \) if and only if
\[
\begin{array}{r}
1+3 rtyuioop+9 fghjklzx+27 vcxzlkjh=0 \\
rtyuioop+6 fghjklzx+27 vcxzlkjh=0 \\
1+6 rtyuioop+45 fghjklzx+270 vcxzlkjh=0
\end{array}
\]

These linear equations have the solution \( rtyuioop=-1, fghjklzx=\frac{1}{3}, vcxzlkjh=-\frac{1}{27} \), so
\[
asdfghjk-ghjklasd+\frac{1}{3} uioplkjh-\frac{1}{27} xcvbnmas=0
\]

Let \( mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)=lakdjfgh+qwerpoiu qzxwvtnp+zmxncvas qzxwvtnp^{2}+\cdots \). Proceeding formally, we have
\[
\begin{array}{rlrl}
qzxwvtnp^{3} mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) & = & lakdjfgh qzxwvtnp^{3}+qwerpoiu qzxwvtnp^{4}+\cdots+wertyuii qzxwvtnp^{fpldsear}+\cdots \\
rtyuioop qzxwvtnp^{2} mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) & = & rtyuioop lakdjfgh qzxwvtnp^{2}+rtyuioop qwerpoiu qzxwvtnp^{3}+rtyuioop zmxncvas qzxwvtnp^{4}+\cdots+rtyuioop sdfghjkk qzxwvtnp^{fpldsear}+\cdots \\
fghjklzx qzxwvtnp mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) & = & fghjklzx lakdjfgh qzxwvtnp+fghjklzx qwerpoiu qzxwvtnp^{2}+fghjklzx zmxncvas qzxwvtnp^{3}+fghjklzx bnmhytre qzxwvtnp^{4}+\cdots+fghjklzx cvbnmqqw qzxwvtnp^{fpldsear}+\cdots \\
vcxzlkjh mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) & = & vcxzlkjh lakdjfgh+vcxzlkjh qwerpoiu qzxwvtnp+vcxzlkjh zmxncvas qzxwvtnp^{2}+vcxzlkjh bnmhytre qzxwvtnp^{3}+vcxzlkjh plokijuh qzxwvtnp^{4}+\cdots+vcxzlkjh asdfghjk qzxwvtnp^{fpldsear}+\cdots
\end{array}
\]

When we sum these we get
\[
mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)\left[qzxwvtnp^{3}+rtyuioop qzxwvtnp^{2}+fghjklzx qzxwvtnp+vcxzlkjh\right]=\left(rtyuioop lakdjfgh+fghjklzx qwerpoiu+vcxzlkjh zmxncvas\right) qzxwvtnp^{2}+\left(fghjklzx lakdjfgh+vcxzlkjh qwerpoiu\right) qzxwvtnp+vcxzlkjh lakdjfgh
\]

Multiplying through by -27 , we obtain
\[
mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)\left[1-9 qzxwvtnp+27 qzxwvtnp^{2}-27 qzxwvtnp^{3}\right]=1-3 qzxwvtnp+18 qzxwvtnp^{2}
\]
and therefore
\[
mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)=\frac{1-3 qzxwvtnp+18 qzxwvtnp^{2}}{(1-3 qzxwvtnp)^{3}}
\]

Using the ratio test we conclude that the series converges for \( |qzxwvtnp|<\frac{1}{3} \); hence the formal manipulations above are valid for these values of \( qzxwvtnp \).

Second Solution. Let \( lkjhgfdx=asdfghjk / 3^{fpldsear}=fpldsear^{2}+1 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta lkjhgfdx & =poiuytre-lkjhgfdx=2 fpldsear+1, \quad \Delta^{2} lkjhgfdx=mnbvcxzq-2 poiuytre+lkjhgfdx=2 \\
\Delta^{3} lkjhgfdx & =zxcvbnmm-3 mnbvcxzq+3 poiuytre-lkjhgfdx=0
\end{aligned}
\]

So
\[
\frac{xcvbnmas}{3^{fpldsear+3}}-\frac{3 uioplkjh}{3^{fpldsear+2}}+\frac{3 ghjklasd}{3^{fpldsear+1}}-\frac{asdfghjk}{3^{fpldsear}}=0
\]
whence
\[
asdfghjk-ghjklasd+(1 / 3) uioplkjh-(1 / 27) xcvbnmas=0
\]

Since
\[
fpldsear^{2}+1=(fpldsear+1)(fpldsear+2)-3(fpldsear+1)+2
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma lkjhgfdx hjgrksla^{fpldsear} & =\Sigma(fpldsear+1)(fpldsear+2) hjgrksla^{fpldsear}-3 \Sigma(fpldsear+1) hjgrksla^{fpldsear}+2 \Sigma hjgrksla^{fpldsear} \\
& =\frac{2}{(1-hjgrksla)^{3}}-\frac{3}{(1-hjgrksla)^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-hjgrksla} \\
& =\frac{1-hjgrksla+2 hjgrksla^{2}}{(1-hjgrksla)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
\]
provided \( |hjgrksla|<1 \). Replace \( hjgrksla \) by \( 3 qzxwvtnp \).
\[
\Sigma asdfghjk qzxwvtnp^{fpldsear}=\frac{1-3 qzxwvtnp+18 qzxwvtnp^{2}}{(1-3 qzxwvtnp)^{3}}
\]
provided \( |qzxwvtnp|<\frac{1}{3} \).
Remark. We could assume that the problem is concerned with the ring of formal power series. In that case, \( (1-3 qzxwvtnp) \) has an inverse in the ring and our result is that
\[
\sum asdfghjk qzxwvtnp^{fpldsear}=\left(1-3 qzxwvtnp+18 qzxwvtnp^{2}\right)(1-3 qzxwvtnp)^{-3}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
a_{n}=n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)\,2^{n}\;+\;\bigl(n^{2}+1\bigr)(-3)^{n}\qquad(n\ge 0)
\]
and let its ordinary generating function be  
\[
S(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}\,x^{n}.
\]

1.\;(Linear annihilator)\;Prove that there exist constants  
\[
c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{8}\in\mathbb{C},\qquad c_{0}\neq 0,
\]
independent of $n$, such that for every $n\ge 0$
\[
c_{0}a_{n+8}+c_{1}a_{n+7}+\dots +c_{7}a_{n+1}+c_{8}a_{n}=0.
\]

2.\;(Minimal order \& exact coefficients)  
a)\;Show that no relation of order $\le 7$ is possible, so the relation in (1) is of minimal length.  
b)\;Determine the unique integer coefficients (up to a common non-zero factor) and write the recurrence explicitly.

3.\;(Closed rational form)  
Prove directly from the definition of $a_{n}$ that $S(x)$ is annihilated by the polynomial $(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}$ and hence
\[
S(x)=\frac{P(x)}{(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}},
\qquad\deg P\le 7.
\]
Compute the polynomial $P(x)$ explicitly.

4.\;(Analytic information)  
Locate every singularity of $S(x)$ in $\mathbb{C}$ and determine the exact radius of convergence of the power series.

5.\;(Numerical evaluation)  
Evaluate the convergent series
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{a_{n}}{6^{n}}
\]
in closed form.

\vspace{.5em}
kernel_variant.solution:
1.\;Existence of an order-$8$ linear relation  

Let $E$ be the forward-shift operator, $(E\,f)_{n}=f_{n+1}$.

(i)\;$n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)\,2^{n}$ is a degree-$4$ polynomial in $n$ multiplied by $2^{n}$, hence it is annihilated by $(E-2)^{5}$ (the $5^{\text{th}}$ difference of a degree-$4$ polynomial evaluated at $2^{n}$ vanishes).

(ii)\;$(n^{2}+1)(-3)^{n}$ is a degree-$2$ polynomial times $(-3)^{n}$, hence it is annihilated by $(E+3)^{3}$.

Since $(E-2)^{5}\,(E+3)^{3}$ annihilates each summand, it annihilates their sum $a_{n}$.  
Expanding the product gives a monic polynomial of degree $8$ in $E$, so an order-$8$ homogeneous linear recurrence with constant coefficients exists.

2.\;The minimal recurrence and its coefficients  

a)\;Minimality  

The factors $E-2$ and $E+3$ are coprime as polynomials in $E$.  
For any linear operator $L(E)$ with constant coefficients that annihilates $a_{n}$, we have
\[
L(E)=U(E)\,(E-2)^{5}(E+3)^{3},
\]
because $(E-2)^{5}(E+3)^{3}$ is a greatest common right-divisor of the two annihilators obtained in 1(i) and 1(ii).  
(One may invoke Bezout's identity for polynomials: since $\gcd(E-2,E+3)=1$ there exist polynomials $A,B$ with $A(E-2)+B(E+3)=1$, and hence any common multiple of $(E-2)^{5}$ and $(E+3)^{3}$ is divisible by their product.)  
Therefore $\deg L\ge 5+3=8$, so no recurrence of order $\le 7$ can exist; the order $8$ found above is minimal.

b)\;Explicit coefficients  

The recurrence coefficients are those of the polynomial
\[
(E-2)^{5}(E+3)^{3}\;=\;\sum_{k=0}^{8}c_{k}\,E^{8-k},
\]
read from highest to lowest degree.  Equivalently, they are the coefficients of
\[
(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}=\sum_{k=0}^{8}c_{k}\,x^{k}.
\]
Compute  
\[
\begin{aligned}
(1-2x)^{5}&=1-10x+40x^{2}-80x^{3}+80x^{4}-32x^{5},\\
(1+3x)^{3}&=1+9x+27x^{2}+27x^{3},
\end{aligned}
\]
then multiply:
\[
\bigl(1-2x\bigr)^{5}\bigl(1+3x\bigr)^{3}
=1- x-23x^{2}+37x^{3}+170x^{4}-392x^{5}-288x^{6}+1296x^{7}-864x^{8}.
\]
Hence, for every $n\ge 0$,
\[
\boxed{%
a_{n+8}-a_{n+7}-23a_{n+6}+37a_{n+5}+170a_{n+4}
-392a_{n+3}-288a_{n+2}+1296a_{n+1}-864a_{n}=0}.
\]

3.\;Closed rational form of $S(x)$  

Separate $a_{n}$ into two parts:

(i)\;$\displaystyle \sum_{n\ge 0}n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)\,2^{n}x^{n}$

\[
=\frac{4!\,(2x)^{4}}{(1-2x)^{5}}
=\frac{384\,x^{4}}{(1-2x)^{5}}.
\]

(ii)\;$\displaystyle \sum_{n\ge 0}(n^{2}+1)(-3)^{n}x^{n}$  

Write $n^{2}+1=n(n-1)+n+1$ and use the standard formulae for the sums of powers:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n\ge 0}n(n-1)(-3x)^{n}&=\frac{2(-3x)^{2}}{(1+3x)^{3}},\\
\sum_{n\ge 0}n(-3x)^{n}&=\frac{-3x}{(1+3x)^{2}},\\
\sum_{n\ge 0}(-3x)^{n}&=\frac{1}{1+3x}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Adding gives
\[
\sum_{n\ge 0}(n^{2}+1)(-3x)^{n}
=\frac{1+3x+18x^{2}}{(1+3x)^{3}}.
\]

Add (i) and (ii) over the common denominator $(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
P(x)&=384x^{4}(1+3x)^{3}+(1+3x+18x^{2})(1-2x)^{5}\\[2pt]
&=1-7x+28x^{2}-140x^{3}+944x^{4}\\
&\quad +2224x^{5}+11712x^{6}+9792x^{7}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
\boxed{%
S(x)=\dfrac{1-7x+28x^{2}-140x^{3}+944x^{4}+2224x^{5}+11712x^{6}+9792x^{7}}
{(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}} }.
\]

4.\;Singularities and radius of convergence  

$S(x)$ is a rational function.  Its only singularities are the poles coming from the denominator:

\[
x=\frac12\quad(\text{order }5),\qquad x=-\frac13\quad(\text{order }3).
\]

Hence the power series about $x=0$ converges in the largest open disc avoiding these poles, namely

\[
R=\min\Bigl\{\bigl|\,\tfrac12\bigr|,\bigl|-\tfrac13\bigr|\Bigr\}=\frac13.
\]

Therefore the series converges for $|x|<\dfrac13$ and diverges for $|x|>\dfrac13$.  
(Only two points on the circle $|x|=\dfrac13$ are singular; analytic continuation is possible through every other point of the circle, so the circle is the boundary of convergence but not a natural boundary in the classical sense.)

5.\;Numerical evaluation at $x=\dfrac16$  

Because $\dfrac16<R$, substitution into the closed form is legitimate.

\[
\begin{aligned}
(1-2x)^{5}&=\Bigl(1-\frac13\Bigr)^{5}=\Bigl(\frac23\Bigr)^{5}=\frac{32}{243},\\
(1+3x)^{3}&=\Bigl(1+\frac12\Bigr)^{3}=\Bigl(\frac32\Bigr)^{3}=\frac{27}{8}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Compute the two partial sums exactly:

(i)\;First part  
\[
\frac{384x^{4}}{(1-2x)^{5}}
=\frac{384\left(\dfrac16\right)^{4}}{\dfrac{32}{243}}
=\frac{8/27}{32/243}=\frac{9}{4}.
\]

(ii)\;Second part  
\[
\frac{1+3x+18x^{2}}{(1+3x)^{3}}
=\frac{1+\dfrac12+\dfrac12}{27/8}
=\frac{2}{27/8}=\frac{16}{27}.
\]

Adding yields
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{a_{n}}{6^{n}}
=S\!\bigl(\tfrac16\bigr)=\frac94+\frac{16}{27}=\frac{307}{108}.}
\]

\vspace{.5em}

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher degree & mixed bases The first summand involves a degree-4 polynomial while the second uses a degree-2 polynomial and the opposite sign base (−3).  Their combination forces an 8-term recurrence, double the length of the original variant.

• Multiple distinct characteristic roots Because 2 and −3 are coprime roots, students must factor and combine two high-multiplicity annihilators, recognise coprimality and minimality, and handle much larger computations.

• Algebraic heft Expanding (1−2x)⁵(1+3x)³ and constructing P(x) entails manipulating polynomials up to degree 8, far more cumbersome than the quadratic–cubic case of the original.

• Analytic component Locating all complex singularities and proving the radius of convergence injects complex-analysis reasoning absent from the original.

• Numerical evaluation A non-trivial rational value (307/108) must be distilled from the rational function, demanding careful arithmetic and checking.

Overall the variant intertwines high-order difference operators, generating-function calculus, polynomial algebra, and analytic considerations, substantially deepening both the conceptual and computational load compared with the current kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
a_{n}=n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)\,2^{n}\;+\;\bigl(n^{2}+1\bigr)(-3)^{n}\qquad(n\ge 0)
\]
and let its ordinary generating function be  
\[
S(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}\,x^{n}.
\]

1.\;(Linear annihilator)\;Prove that there exist constants  
\[
c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{8}\in\mathbb{C},\qquad c_{0}\neq 0,
\]
independent of $n$, such that for every $n\ge 0$
\[
c_{0}a_{n+8}+c_{1}a_{n+7}+\dots +c_{7}a_{n+1}+c_{8}a_{n}=0.
\]

2.\;(Minimal order \& exact coefficients)  
a)\;Show that no relation of order $\le 7$ is possible, so the relation in (1) is of minimal length.  
b)\;Determine the unique integer coefficients (up to a common non-zero factor) and write the recurrence explicitly.

3.\;(Closed rational form)  
Prove directly from the definition of $a_{n}$ that $S(x)$ is annihilated by the polynomial $(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}$ and hence
\[
S(x)=\frac{P(x)}{(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}},
\qquad\deg P\le 7.
\]
Compute the polynomial $P(x)$ explicitly.

4.\;(Analytic information)  
Locate every singularity of $S(x)$ in $\mathbb{C}$ and determine the exact radius of convergence of the power series.

5.\;(Numerical evaluation)  
Evaluate the convergent series
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{a_{n}}{6^{n}}
\]
in closed form.

\vspace{.5em}
original_kernel_variant.solution:
1.\;Existence of an order-$8$ linear relation  

Let $E$ be the forward-shift operator, $(E\,f)_{n}=f_{n+1}$.

(i)\;$n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)\,2^{n}$ is a degree-$4$ polynomial in $n$ multiplied by $2^{n}$, hence it is annihilated by $(E-2)^{5}$ (the $5^{\text{th}}$ difference of a degree-$4$ polynomial evaluated at $2^{n}$ vanishes).

(ii)\;$(n^{2}+1)(-3)^{n}$ is a degree-$2$ polynomial times $(-3)^{n}$, hence it is annihilated by $(E+3)^{3}$.

Since $(E-2)^{5}\,(E+3)^{3}$ annihilates each summand, it annihilates their sum $a_{n}$.  
Expanding the product gives a monic polynomial of degree $8$ in $E$, so an order-$8$ homogeneous linear recurrence with constant coefficients exists.

2.\;The minimal recurrence and its coefficients  

a)\;Minimality  

The factors $E-2$ and $E+3$ are coprime as polynomials in $E$.  
For any linear operator $L(E)$ with constant coefficients that annihilates $a_{n}$, we have
\[
L(E)=U(E)\,(E-2)^{5}(E+3)^{3},
\]
because $(E-2)^{5}(E+3)^{3}$ is a greatest common right-divisor of the two annihilators obtained in 1(i) and 1(ii).  
(One may invoke Bezout's identity for polynomials: since $\gcd(E-2,E+3)=1$ there exist polynomials $A,B$ with $A(E-2)+B(E+3)=1$, and hence any common multiple of $(E-2)^{5}$ and $(E+3)^{3}$ is divisible by their product.)  
Therefore $\deg L\ge 5+3=8$, so no recurrence of order $\le 7$ can exist; the order $8$ found above is minimal.

b)\;Explicit coefficients  

The recurrence coefficients are those of the polynomial
\[
(E-2)^{5}(E+3)^{3}\;=\;\sum_{k=0}^{8}c_{k}\,E^{8-k},
\]
read from highest to lowest degree.  Equivalently, they are the coefficients of
\[
(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}=\sum_{k=0}^{8}c_{k}\,x^{k}.
\]
Compute  
\[
\begin{aligned}
(1-2x)^{5}&=1-10x+40x^{2}-80x^{3}+80x^{4}-32x^{5},\\
(1+3x)^{3}&=1+9x+27x^{2}+27x^{3},
\end{aligned}
\]
then multiply:
\[
\bigl(1-2x\bigr)^{5}\bigl(1+3x\bigr)^{3}
=1- x-23x^{2}+37x^{3}+170x^{4}-392x^{5}-288x^{6}+1296x^{7}-864x^{8}.
\]
Hence, for every $n\ge 0$,
\[
\boxed{%
a_{n+8}-a_{n+7}-23a_{n+6}+37a_{n+5}+170a_{n+4}
-392a_{n+3}-288a_{n+2}+1296a_{n+1}-864a_{n}=0}.
\]

3.\;Closed rational form of $S(x)$  

Separate $a_{n}$ into two parts:

(i)\;$\displaystyle \sum_{n\ge 0}n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)\,2^{n}x^{n}$

\[
=\frac{4!\,(2x)^{4}}{(1-2x)^{5}}
=\frac{384\,x^{4}}{(1-2x)^{5}}.
\]

(ii)\;$\displaystyle \sum_{n\ge 0}(n^{2}+1)(-3)^{n}x^{n}$  

Write $n^{2}+1=n(n-1)+n+1$ and use the standard formulae for the sums of powers:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n\ge 0}n(n-1)(-3x)^{n}&=\frac{2(-3x)^{2}}{(1+3x)^{3}},\\
\sum_{n\ge 0}n(-3x)^{n}&=\frac{-3x}{(1+3x)^{2}},\\
\sum_{n\ge 0}(-3x)^{n}&=\frac{1}{1+3x}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Adding gives
\[
\sum_{n\ge 0}(n^{2}+1)(-3x)^{n}
=\frac{1+3x+18x^{2}}{(1+3x)^{3}}.
\]

Add (i) and (ii) over the common denominator $(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
P(x)&=384x^{4}(1+3x)^{3}+(1+3x+18x^{2})(1-2x)^{5}\\[2pt]
&=1-7x+28x^{2}-140x^{3}+944x^{4}\\
&\quad +2224x^{5}+11712x^{6}+9792x^{7}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
\boxed{%
S(x)=\dfrac{1-7x+28x^{2}-140x^{3}+944x^{4}+2224x^{5}+11712x^{6}+9792x^{7}}
{(1-2x)^{5}(1+3x)^{3}} }.
\]

4.\;Singularities and radius of convergence  

$S(x)$ is a rational function.  Its only singularities are the poles coming from the denominator:

\[
x=\frac12\quad(\text{order }5),\qquad x=-\frac13\quad(\text{order }3).
\]

Hence the power series about $x=0$ converges in the largest open disc avoiding these poles, namely

\[
R=\min\Bigl\{\bigl|\,\tfrac12\bigr|,\bigl|-\tfrac13\bigr|\Bigr\}=\frac13.
\]

Therefore the series converges for $|x|<\dfrac13$ and diverges for $|x|>\dfrac13$.  
(Only two points on the circle $|x|=\dfrac13$ are singular; analytic continuation is possible through every other point of the circle, so the circle is the boundary of convergence but not a natural boundary in the classical sense.)

5.\;Numerical evaluation at $x=\dfrac16$  

Because $\dfrac16<R$, substitution into the closed form is legitimate.

\[
\begin{aligned}
(1-2x)^{5}&=\Bigl(1-\frac13\Bigr)^{5}=\Bigl(\frac23\Bigr)^{5}=\frac{32}{243},\\
(1+3x)^{3}&=\Bigl(1+\frac12\Bigr)^{3}=\Bigl(\frac32\Bigr)^{3}=\frac{27}{8}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Compute the two partial sums exactly:

(i)\;First part  
\[
\frac{384x^{4}}{(1-2x)^{5}}
=\frac{384\left(\dfrac16\right)^{4}}{\dfrac{32}{243}}
=\frac{8/27}{32/243}=\frac{9}{4}.
\]

(ii)\;Second part  
\[
\frac{1+3x+18x^{2}}{(1+3x)^{3}}
=\frac{1+\dfrac12+\dfrac12}{27/8}
=\frac{2}{27/8}=\frac{16}{27}.
\]

Adding yields
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{a_{n}}{6^{n}}
=S\!\bigl(\tfrac16\bigr)=\frac94+\frac{16}{27}=\frac{307}{108}.}
\]

\vspace{.5em}

• Higher degree & mixed bases The first summand involves a degree-4 polynomial while the second uses a degree-2 polynomial and the opposite sign base (−3).  Their combination forces an 8-term recurrence, double the length of the original variant.

• Multiple distinct characteristic roots Because 2 and −3 are coprime roots, students must factor and combine two high-multiplicity annihilators, recognise coprimality and minimality, and handle much larger computations.

• Algebraic heft Expanding (1−2x)⁵(1+3x)³ and constructing P(x) entails manipulating polynomials up to degree 8, far more cumbersome than the quadratic–cubic case of the original.

• Analytic component Locating all complex singularities and proving the radius of convergence injects complex-analysis reasoning absent from the original.

• Numerical evaluation A non-trivial rational value (307/108) must be distilled from the rational function, demanding careful arithmetic and checking.

Overall the variant intertwines high-order difference operators, generating-function calculus, polynomial algebra, and analytic considerations, substantially deepening both the conceptual and computational load compared with the current kernel version.
